name: Ascent to Collado Garcia — circular via Alcudia de Monteagud and Tahal
name_original: Subida hasta el Collado Garcia — circular por Alcudia de Monteagud y Tahal
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Chercos are situated in the Sierra de los Filabres, a mountainous region in Almería province, Spain. The area features a landscape of hills, valleys, and semi-arid terrain, with elevations reaching above 800 meters. Road cyclists can expect varied ascents and descents on quiet, traffic-free roads. The region offers panoramic views across valleys and surrounding villages, providing a challenging environment for cycling. Best road cycling routes around Chercos The most popular road cycling route is Ascent to Collado Garcia — circular via Alcudia de Monteagud and Tahal, a 21.8 miles (35.0 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 2 minutes to complete. This route features a significant ascent and passes through several local villages. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Lucainena de las Torres – Puerto de la Virgen loop from Uleila del Campo, a moderate 35.9 miles (57.7 km) path. This loop offers varied terrain and includes a climb to Puerto de la Virgen. Local road cyclists also love the Old Tíjola train Station – Fuente de las Focas of Macael loop from Macael, a 27.5 miles (44.2 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 16 minutes. Road cycling around Chercos is defined by mountainous terrain, deep valleys, and quiet roads. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels, with significant elevation gains. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Chercos's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Chercos?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes available around Chercos, offering a range of experiences from moderate to difficult. These routes are primarily situated in the mountainous Sierra de los Filabres, providing varied ascents and descents on quiet roads.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling near Chercos?
A: Road cycling near Chercos involves mountainous terrain within the Sierra de los Filabres. You can expect a landscape of hills, valleys, and semi-arid areas, with elevations often exceeding 800 meters. The routes feature varied ascents and descents on quiet, traffic-free roads, offering panoramic views across valleys and surrounding villages.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Chercos offers challenging road cycling routes suitable for experienced riders. Out of the 9 available routes, 2 are classified as difficult, featuring significant elevation gains. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38161789" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tahal Castle – Iron Bridge of Cantoria loop from Uleila del Campo&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult route covering nearly 94 km with over 2000 meters of elevation gain.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or landmarks I can see along the road cycling routes?
A: The region around Chercos is rich in scenic viewpoints and historical landmarks. You can enjoy expansive vistas from various &lt;em&gt;miradores&lt;/em&gt; (viewpoints) throughout the Sierra de los Filabres. Notable attractions include the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5313387" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tahal Castle&lt;/a&gt;, the charming settlement of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5578230" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Uleila del Campo&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4995439" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Virgen de la Cabeza de Monteagud Sanctuary&lt;/a&gt;. The ancient settlement of Chercos Viejo, with its old Arab fortress remains, also offers unique historical sights.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Chercos?
A: Yes, several circular road cycling routes are available. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987666676/ascent-to-collado-garcia-circular-via-alcudia-de-monteagud-and-tahal" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to Collado Garcia — circular via Alcudia de Monteagud and Tahal&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 35 km trail. Another favorite is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33919131" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lucainena de las Torres – Puerto de la Virgen loop from Uleila del Campo&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 57.7 km path that includes a climb to Puerto de la Virgen.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Chercos?
A: The semi-arid climate of the Sierra de los Filabres generally offers good conditions for cycling outside of the hottest summer months. Spring and autumn are typically ideal, providing milder temperatures and pleasant weather for tackling the region's ascents and descents. Winter can also be suitable, though higher elevations may experience cooler temperatures.

Q: Is Chercos a good destination for family-friendly road cycling?
A: While the region is characterized by mountainous terrain and significant elevation changes, making many routes moderate to difficult, families with older, experienced cycling members might find suitable options. However, for families with younger children or beginners, the challenging nature of the climbs and descents might be demanding. It's advisable to check individual route profiles for elevation and distance to ensure suitability.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Chercos?
A: Road cyclists frequently praise the quiet, traffic-free roads and the stunning panoramic views across valleys and surrounding villages. The challenging ascents and descents within the Sierra de los Filabres are also a major draw for those seeking a rewarding workout amidst beautiful, rugged landscapes.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: While specific cafes on every route are not detailed, the routes often pass through local villages such as Alcudia de Monteagud, Tahal, Uleila del Campo, and Macael. These villages typically offer opportunities for refreshments, allowing cyclists to take a break and experience local hospitality.

Q: What are the parking options for cyclists in Chercos?
A: Chercos is a small municipality, and while specific dedicated cyclist parking areas are not highlighted, parking is generally available within the villages that serve as starting points for many routes. It's advisable to check local parking regulations upon arrival in villages like Chercos Nuevo or other nearby towns.

Q: Is public transport available to reach Chercos for cycling?
A: Public transport options directly to Chercos for cyclists might be limited due to its rural, mountainous location. The broader Almería province has some regional bus services, but direct connections to smaller municipalities like Chercos may require transfers or be infrequent. It's often more convenient to travel by car to a starting point in the region.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural points of interest to explore near the cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. Beyond the cycling, the area offers rich historical and cultural sites. You can visit the ancient settlement of Chercos Viejo, with its winding streets and remains of an Arab fortress. The prehistoric archaeological site of Piedra Labrá, featuring rock engravings from the second millennium B.C., is located between Chercos Viejo and Chercos Nuevo. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chercos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;municipality of Chercos&lt;/a&gt; itself holds historical significance.
tips: Radiant white houses in a bizarre semi-desert landscape. An oasis in the beautiful desert.

Tahal Castle, nestled in the Sierra de los Filabres, was built in the 15th and 16th centuries to control the routes between the Almanzora Valley and Tabernas. Although it remains in a state of consolidated ruin today, its large rectangular keep, with its elevated entrance and masonry walls, is still impressive. Next to it, you can see the remains of a circular tower, the walled enclosure, and a cistern that provided water in the event of a siege. Freely accessible, it is an interesting place to combine history and hiking in this part of Almería.
Nice pass (1250m) not only for racing cyclists. Great topping.
